frontmatter.title: Historical Context
frontmatter.description: Women in early Christianity — who they were, why they matter, and why the historical record is incomplete.

children.value: Historical Context: Women in Early Christianity

children.label: Historical Context: Women in Early Christianity

children.value: The first five centuries of Christian history produced a substantial number of women who led communities, developed theology, wrote texts, resolved ecclesiastical disputes, funded institutions, and exercised prophetic authority. Most of them are unknown to a general audience. Many are known only through sources written by men who had reasons — rhetorical, theological, institutional — to shape how they are remembered.
This page provides the historical background necessary for understanding both the figures under investigation and the interpretive challenges the project confronts.
The Documentary Problem

TODO: Expand with discussion of:
- Survival of sources: which texts survived and why
- The patristic canon and its gendered composition
- Apocryphal and Gnostic materials as alternative sources
- Hagiography: conventions and critical reading
- Archaeological evidence (inscriptions, graffiti) as a corrective
Women’s Roles in the First Three Centuries

children.label: Women’s Roles in the First Three Centuries

children.value:
TODO: Expand with discussion of:
- House church leaders (Priscilla, Lydia, Nympha, Chloe)
- Apostles and missionaries (Junia, Mary Magdalene, Thecla)
- Prophets (Philip's daughters, Ammia of Philadelphia, Maximilla, Priscilla)
- Deacons (Phoebe, the women of 1 Tim 3:11)
- Martyrs and confessors (Perpetua, Felicitas, Blandina)
Women’s Roles in the Fourth and Fifth Centuries

children.label: Women’s Roles in the Fourth and Fifth Centuries

children.value:
TODO: Expand with discussion of:
- The institutionalization of the diaconate
- Deaconesses: Olympias, Marthana, the Didascalia and Apostolic Constitutions
- Monastic founders: Macrina, Melania the Elder, Melania the Younger, Paula
- Intellectual patrons: Marcella, Paula, Fabiola
- The narrowing of women's roles after Nicaea and its contested interpretation
Key Scholarly Literature
The following works are foundational for contextualizing the figures under study.
Cohick & Hughes (2017)
— The most comprehensive recent survey of female figures from the second through fifth centuries.
Madigan & Osiek (2005)
— Primary sources in translation with critical commentary on ordained women.
Kraemer & D'Angelo (1999)
— Foundational feminist historiography of the period.
— Scholarly analysis of female asceticism in late antiquity.
Torjesen (1993)
— Argument for women’s formal priestly leadership in early Christianity.
Clark (1983)
— Patristic primary sources on women in translation, with commentary.
Swan (2001)
— Sayings, lives, and stories of early Christian women in the desert tradition.
Oden (1994)
— Anthology of women’s writings across the history of Christian thought.
Paintner (2012)
— Annotated introduction to the sayings of the desert fathers and mothers.

cohick2017women.html: Cohick, L. H., & Hughes, A. B. (2017). Christian Women in the Patristic World: Their Influence, Authority, and Legacy in the Second through Fifth Centuries. Baker Academic.
